Any circle, family, friends, employees, clients, they all have a different perception of you than you think they have of you. They've all focused on different things than you think they've, you know, focused on. The answer to your other question is that as you probably will too, for example, this list, I do every one of them, but the primary for someone new is providing radical revolutionary system. And most of my acquisition is about that. So it is narrowed to the throw out everything you've ever been told about advertising and marketing and, you know, here's the golden path, right, which defies most of what you've been doing. Then probably second for me would be specialist expert, which is where the most money comes from. But ultimately as your relationship deepens, then you have to play 56 different roles with people in order to hold their interest. But people kind of need to be able to like pigeonhole you. They need to be able to... Yeah, they need to like have in their mind what this guy is. And if there's a lot of confusion in their mind about what that is, you know, it's hard to connect with them and it's really hard for them to turn around and tell your story to other people, which of course is optimally what we want, right? So like if you think about your dental practice, right, I mean, the way I would tell your story to other people, so like here's where I have you pigeonholed, right, is as a highly skilled cosmetic dentist who deals with extremely difficult cases or extremely difficult patients. So for better or worse, if somebody in Macedonia, Ohio just wanted to ask me how to get a better dental, I might not refer them to you. I might not try and convince them to get on an airplane and fly to you to have their dental work done. But if somebody had that question plus they were extremely phobic or they had TMJ problems or they had, you know, monstrous headaches all the time or they now... Instantly in my mind, oh, that person needs to go to Charlie, right? Now whether that's what you want or not is an entirely different question, but that's where like I have you compartmentalized in my head, right? And so if that serves you well, see then that colors this point of view, it colors every story you tell, it colors everything so that people get that as their primary understanding of who you are and where you're coming from. Then I'm on 40 now. The other part of point of view is what you are and then what you stand for, which gets us to philosophy because ultimately people are looking for values connections, not again resume connections or mechanical connections. So people want to know, they may not enunciate that they want to know it. Sometimes they do, sometimes they don't, but they want to know what you stand for. The YouTube debates, if you've paid attention to them where they're letting the... And of course they're trying to rig them as much as possible, but still where they're letting the public ask the candidates questions. So the Republican, of course that debate is the audience is made up of conservative Republicans in Iowa, so there's a certain closed loop there, but so the Republican debate, which was last night or the night before, I forget which, monstrous criticism amongst the pundits about the questions asked and the candidates willingness to answer them because the bulk of them were really religious litmus test questions. And they weren't policy questions and of course the guy who came out the best was Huckabee because he's a minister. So great questions for him, not such good questions for Say Rudy, but what's instructive about it is, is that none of the pros like the questions and would have asked the questions because the pros are looking for what are you going to do and how the hell are you going to do it, right? And so if you tell me you're going to eliminate the IRS, I want you to tell me how the hell you're going to eliminate the IRS, right? What the pros really don't understand is the public doesn't care about that shit, okay? That's not how they make their decision. Maybe it should be how, but that's not it. So the public's questions, what they're really trying to get at is what does this guy stand for philosophically and do we kind of agree, maybe not perfectly match, but are we somewhat in tune here on what we believe about and the key hell family, in this case religion, money? You know, so like a small business owner sitting in that audience, right? What is he looking, what is he trying to decide between you and him and him? He's not so much trying to figure out what your plans are. He's trying to figure out whether there's some value connection here. So the small business owner, for example, enormously values independence and work ethic and pretty much believes still if you work hard and play by the rules in America, you can be successful. So he's looking, does this person believe that, right? That's what he's looking for. And so when you do point of view, you're dealing with trying to define what you are and then trying to define what you stand for. People want to know. And philosophy is an important part of influence. If you, yeah. My question is, how far do you go? I mean, let me use an extreme example. For instance, let's say that you believe the country would be better if women couldn't drive her boat. I mean, would you say that? Well... Once a while. That's that. Well, let me tell you something sort of interesting about that. No, I wouldn't, of course, in a political context. I would if 80% plus of my best customers were males who were likely to believe the same. Okay? I mean, well, let me give you an equally weird and controversial position, if you will, from the first Republican debate. Eight of the Republican presidential candidates stated they don't believe in evolution. They believe the literal spontaneous creation in seven days and they set it on the stage. Now, in a general election, that's coming back to haunt them if they happen to be the nominee. For the audience, they were playing two at the moment. And of course, in this case, this is a sequential process. If you don't win Iowa and you don't win New Hampshire, you're probably out of the game. So for the audience they were playing two, it was perfect. For the interviewer was the moderator was like shell shocked and like kind of wanted to give them a second chance to correct their position and the audience was applauding. Now, most of us probably would agree that's every bit as weird as the position that you just wanted to advance. But for who they were in front of, that's what they believe. Say, so there's a principle I'm going to come to that the easiest way to influence people is of course to be in complete agreement with what they are with what they most believe. And so there's a place for that. I mean, think about gun positions, which you're very familiar with. So think about Ted Nugent and why is he so popular with the gun hunting crowd because of his extreme positions? So no, I probably wouldn't take that position if I was trying to win a general election. You know, but I don't know if I was running for town council in Dumpas, South Carolina, where they still fly the Confederate flag and you know, a lot of people probably believe that you could probably get elected running on that.
